[JS Weird World] No.68 Wrapping Up the Weird Parts of JavaScript
JsWeirdWorld
JavaScript
JavaScript 全攻略:克服JS奇怪的部分

Finishing the weird parts of JavaScript feels like I finally kept a promise to myself — keep learning. But every ending is really another beginning. So maybe I'll come back, go through the bits I'm still fuzzy on, and write them down all over again. Here's a short reflection on the whole series.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/25/js-weird-world-67-adding-jquery-support/
[JS Weird World] No.67 Adding jQuery Support to Our Mini Framework

Next up, we wire jQuery into the little framework we've been building. We'll add an HTMLGreeting method to the prototype that drops a greeting straight into the DOM through a jQuery selector — and guard against jQuery not being loaded. Here's how it comes together.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/25/jsweirdworld-66-properties-and-chainable-methods/
[JS Weird World] No.66 Properties and Chainable Methods

Next up we're adding some features and properties to our Greeter, plus a few default settings. We'll use closures to keep shared data private, give the prototype some methods anyone can call, and copy jQuery's chainable style so you can keep dotting one method after another.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-65-object-and-its-prototype/
[JS Weird World] No.65 Our Object and Its Prototype
JavaScript Full Guide: Conquering the Weird Parts of JS

Now we're getting ready to set up the Greeter object so we can make it behave as close to jQuery as possible. It takes a little trick, though. In this post we wire up the constructor, hook up the prototype chain, and attach the whole thing to the global scope so you can call it with G$().
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-63-greetr-requirements/
[JS Weird World] No.63 Building Our Own Framework: The Requirements

Now that we've got a rough handle on jQuery, it's time to roll up our sleeves and build a little framework of our own. Before we write any code, let's lay out exactly what GREETR needs to do — the greetings, the language support, and the jQuery integration we're aiming for.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-64-safe-code/
[JS Weird World] No.64 Building Safe, Reusable Code

Now that we've got the requirements sorted out, it's time to make our little framework safe to reuse in any JavaScript environment. In this post we wrap everything in an IIFE and hand it the global window and jQuery objects, so our variables stay protected and don't leak out.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-62-reading-jquery-source/
[JS Weird World] No.62 Reading the jQuery Source Code to Learn JavaScript

In this one we use jQuery to learn JavaScript — we'll look at how it's structured and how to use it. The goal isn't to understand how every single feature works under the hood. It's to see whether we can actually read real source code, and to learn from how it's put together.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-61-learn-from-good-code/
[JS Weird World] No.61 Learning From Good Code

There are plenty of ways to become a better programmer, but honestly the best and most effective one is learning from code other people wrote. In this post I'll talk about where to find good code to learn from, and why reading the source of well-known libraries like jQuery is a great place to start.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-60-strict-mode/
[JS Weird World] No.60 Strict Mode: Make JavaScript Stop Covering for Your Typos

After the last few chapters, you've probably noticed how loose JavaScript can be. But if you want it to be stricter, that's totally doable. There's a single line you can add to flip JavaScript into strict mode — and it'll stop quietly covering for the kind of typos that cause nasty bugs.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/24/js-weird-world-59-typeof-instanceof/
[JS Weird World] No.59 typeof, instanceof, and Figuring Out What Something Actually Is

We already know JavaScript is a dynamically typed language. That lets us do some pretty special things, but it's also dangerous. Normally when we want to know a variable's type, we reach for typeof — but honestly that method isn't great. So this chapter walks through typeof and instanceof, and how to actually figure out what something is.
